speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number seven, interview with Eva Roden. Unfortunately, at the close of tape number six, our tape jammed. So we were not able to complete some of the stories you were telling us. And I'll bring you back to where we were.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Please.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had just arrived in Bergen-Belsen and had had a terrible journey to Bergen-Belsen. And you were beginning to tell us about the conditions, the living conditions, in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- they marched us into camp. It was a very hot day for that time of the year. It was-- the whole of 1945 was a very, very hot spring. That was very fortunate for us. But when we walked into Belsen, it was a living nightmare. It was like-- like hell personified, because A, they didn't bury dead in Belsen. I don't know for how long. They said since Christmas. I gladly believe it. There were dead bodies lying absolutely everywhere strewn over the camp. They were in all shapes of form. People didn't register them-- the live people-- the people who were alive. They sat on them. They relieved themselves on them. And they-- they it was like-- like a-- like Dante's I-- I suppose. I don't know. And then, we-- they were-- we were put into a barrack where we-- they were-- that was so overcrowded that there was no way to sleep. There was just a way maybe to sit leaning on each other. And they didn't give us any food. And there was no water in Belsen, absolutely none-- no water to wash or no water to relieve yourself. There was, period, no water. And once, every two, three days, somebody had water and food. And they gave us a thin gruel and a little bit of water to-- to sustain us. But people were dying in front of us like flies, and dropping off, and staying where they dropped. Nobody looked after them. Nobody took them-- nobody even bothered to push them aside. There was a big tent in Belsen, which-- I'm digressing-- which the British found when they liberated us-- a circus tent where they had piles and piles of criss-crossed bodies. I don't know how many-- rotting, literally rotting, covered by-- by a tent. And we couldn't have lasted very long there. That was-- five, six weeks that we were there, I think was the-- the maximum that anybody could last there. And we heard sort of a rumbling of guns in the background the last week. And one day, the SS men, the SS men and women, left. And the next day, they were back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had no idea what was going on. And on a Sunday afternoon-- on a warm Sunday afternoon in April-- yes, we found out later it was the 15th of April-- our SS woman came to the-- to this barrack. And said she needs three or four able-bodied persons who would help her carry some furniture to the gate. And I-- Ruth and I looked at each other. And she said, what can happen? Let's go and carry the-- the furniture. So she had chairs. We each carried a chair. And we come to the-- close to the gate. And in drives a vehicle or car, what you want, a military car. And it has a five-pronged star painted on its side. And I say to Ruth, what will they think of next? Why the star now?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, I don't know. And then, out of the car came a voice with an amplifier who said, you have just been liberated. They said it in German and Polish. You have just been liberated by the 2nd Army, and British Army, and General Hughes. And because there are infectious diseases present, we beg you not to run away and to stay in place. You will be fed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they repeated it in German and Polish, and Polish and German. And of course, bedlam broke out, you know. From all the barracks far and wide, there were-- people came running. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they gathered up strengths. And they were running towards this car. And they were over-- the British were absolutely flabbergasted and overwhelmed. They had no idea what they'll find here. They had no idea there was a camp even there. It was hidden on a sort of a plateau in the woods. And they met somebody who told them there was a camp, and they should go there. They didn't know how to deal with it. That same night, we each got-- they opened a depot with-- of the German food stores. And we each got a tin, a pound tin of lard and a loaf of freshly baked bread, which the Americans baked for us that were stationed some 15 miles away from there. And they gave it to us. And they didn't help much, because with the fresh bread and the tins of lard, about 10,000 people died. You know, no-- they couldn't digest it. And they-- and the next day, they realized the situation. They came with-- they were wonderful. They came with trucks with water and with hot water. And they announced over the loudspeakers that you can come to such and such place, that you will be showered. And you'll get a towel, and you'll get soap. And they were always covered under a sort of tent. And we-- we went. And we were showered. And the-- the British soldiers cried over us. And then, we realized how-- how we must look to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you look to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were skinny. We were completely skinny. We were-- I weighed about 37 kilos or something like that. It-- we were starved and skinny. We were like-- like the people from camps like to see the pictures. And they-- they realized soon that they cannot deal with-- with this Belsen, because it was full of lice, and full of typhus, and the typhoid fever, and full of cadavers, and full of everything. So they came-- the next Sunday, they came with bulldozers. And they dug a huge grave. You probably have seen it on television. There's always the same picture that they show. And they brought in mayors from all over the place, from Hanover and from every village in a-- in a-- I don't know-- 50 miles radius to watch what they did. And the SS people-- whom they caught complete off guard, everybody, everything single one, nobody was-- could run away-- had to bury the-- take care of the dead, you know, throw them into the-- into the mass grave. And then, a priest, and a rabbi, and a minister said the Kaddish, or the appropriate prayer, before they covered it with earth. And then, they decided that the people who have-- we, Ruth and I moved out of the barracks. And we moved onto a watch tower, which has a platform before you go on top of the what. And there we slept. It was very hot and very wonderful. It was really-- this was our luck that it could be so hot in April already. And they-- everything was announced always over the loudspeakers in German and Polish. Obviously, there were so many Poles there. And German was the universal language. And they said that whoever wants to go home and has a place to go to return to, so they'll be collected by nationalities. And who has no place to go or doesn't want to go home, so they assigned them a place where they should gather. And there were-- and Belsen was the camp. And Bergen was a lazarette, an SS lazarette where people were-- SS people were recuperating. They were one-story villas-- beautiful-- they surrounded it with flowers and everything around the little square. And they said the Czechs were going on the-- why I remember this, I don't know. On the 24th of April, the Czechs should be ready. They'll be collected. All Czechs, they should stand in front of barrack, I don't know, 17, for argument's sake. I have no idea that I'm making up this-- but in front of a barrack. And that night, I started to get sick. And I just-- I was getting sick by the minute. I had constant-- I had diarrhea. And I was burning up with fever. And the next day when the truck came to collect us-- so there was an English doctor and a German-- I mean, an English-- two English doctors. One of them spoke German. And they said, we can't take you. Don't worry. They'll be an ambulance which will pick you up. Your are too sick to be taken. And he marked something on my forehead. And they left. And that was one of my scariest moments at the end of the war, because I-- I was left there lying on the ground. And there was nobody, absolutely nobody around me-- not a soul. And I didn't know where I was. And-- and there was a window. And in the reflection of the window, I saw that they marked TY on my forehead. And I fainted. I thought, this is the end. And when I came to, I was-- [LAUGHS] --lying in a-- first, they took me somewhere. And I remember vaguely that somebody was taking off my clothes, dress, and scrubbing me and powdering me. It was DDT. They were nurses. And when I woke-- so I woke up in a bed next to a stove. And there was a British solider feeding the stove. And I was-- I didn't know where I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said-- and I said I was terribly thirsty. And he said-- I said, [NON-ENGLISH]-- German. And then, I said thirst. And he said, no, no. He's not allowed to give me anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I had a-- heard the faucet dripping. So I crawled on all fours to this faucet. It was terrible. And I drank. And there-- there they found me on the floor. But then, I was lucky. I-- there was a nurse from the Red Cross who march-- was walking through the room. I was very sick. And nobody thought I would make it. And one day, there was this nurse marching through the room asking if anybody spoke a little English. So I said I spoke English after a fashion. And she said, that's wonderful. And she moved me into a small room with three other girls. And I'll recuperate much faster. And-- and she made me translate for all the help that was there, because they didn't have any help. And so the-- the Polish and Ukrainian women who were not really prisoners, they were on forced labor there when they fight in the vicinity. So they were acting as nurses or helping the nurses until they could get reinforcement from England and from America. And the-- the first sentence she made me translate in all languages I knew was keep the bed pans always empty. And-- [LAUGHS] --they had no medicine to give us. And they-- they just didn't give us anything. They gave us dry milk powder and maybe some aspirins-- nothing. So you just managed on your own. Your body managed. And so then one day, I-- I was not allowed-- I was-- it was to a quarantine. And then, I knew that Ruth didn't have typhoid and that she was well. And she found an opening in the wire. And the-- the-- we were-- because the quarantine hospitals were behind wires so that nobody could go there. And she came to visit me. And we-- she said, if they-- if there is a transport going to Prague, she'll come for me. She'll fetch me so that we can go together so that I cannot-- that-- we-- because I won't stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva, you've mentioned Ruth many times. But you've never really told us who she was.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a friend of mine-- very good friend of mine. And we really survived this whole business together. We shared everything together. We slept next to each other. And we sort of supported each other. And I don't think either of us would have made it without the other. So that-- that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her family name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruth Bondy, B-O-N-D-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still in touch with her today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She's in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyhow, what happened-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --what happened after that, we are all-- we are already allowed to go a little bit for a walk. And this Elizabeth, this British nurse, took us-- took me to-- the-- the girls in the room took us to this joint, or whatever it was, to this clothes station where they outfitted us as best as they could. And we were allowed to eat a little bit. We-- not much, but still a little bit-- and then-- and then, we knew the war was over. Because of this-- the-- I told you that on Monday, this Hungarian who was distributing the milk came in one morning with the big container of milk, plonked it on the floor and said, [GERMAN], which means German is finished. The war is over, and I'm going home. And do with it-- and do with the milk what you want. And he marched out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was-- there was great rejoicing. And we were very happy. And we didn't know what will happen after this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, one evening, Ruth came in. And she said, come on. Come. Get dressed, and come with me. There is a registration for a transport to Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, at 9 o'clock at night? It was-- we already knew the time. But it was-- it was May, end of May. You know, it was light. So I-- she said, yes, now. So I got dressed into this. And she--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] --she found somewhere a little silver ribbon-- like you tie Champagne bottles with. I don't know where she found it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, you must tie this in your hair so that you look better. I said, why should I look better? She said, they might not take you to the transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we ran. And we crawled under the wire, because it was still quarantine. And then, she said-- then we were running. She said, we have to hurry up. Run.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were-- I couldn't run. She said, but run. And promise me one thing-- that you won't be hysterical. I said, why should I be hysterical? I want to go home. She said, but you promise. So I said, if you-- if I must, I promise. You promise you won't be hysterical? I promise. So then they-- she took me to the barrack. And there was Ruda. And then, I fainted. And that was the end. Or the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the end. It was incredible. And the nicest thing is that when we finally got to Prague, and we got later-- my father was standing in front of this apartment house where which he got for us. He-- he didn't-- he wasn't surprised that he found me. He wasn't surprised that I was alive, that we came back. He just looked.

speaker: interviewer
text: He knew that-- that was going to look for me-- just looked at us. And he said, where were you so long?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the end. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's my story in a nutshell. What did you and Ruda do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After what?

speaker: interviewer
text: After the day you arrived back in Prague. What happened following that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: You remained in Prague for a while?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? We-- we had a very nice apartment, what you would call a studio today, which was furnished completely. You know, there was-- the Germans were running. So whoever came back-- there was an office for repatriated prisoners. So you got an apartment in a very nice modern house. And it was-- it was a studio-- a kitchenette and bed sitting room, completely furnished. I was always afraid that-- the name of the woman after whom we got this apartment-- her name was Gertruda-- that she'll come and fetch it-- do something to us in the middle of the night. But she didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father insisted that we get married, because we-- he-- he said, you have no papers that you are married. Who believes it that you have-- that you're married in Teresin? It's not official.

speaker: interviewee
text: And no daughter of mine with live with somebody under one roof unless she's married. So we got married-- [LAUGHS] --on the 15th of June. Honestly! [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: There's a picture of it here in the big tableau. So you've been married twice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, that-- and then, we went to this house in the country where Ruda's mother is from, where his grandfather lived who has-- was the biggest influence, I think, in his life. And all the people-- the house was there. There was nobody in the house. And all the people in the village were wonderful to us. And we-- and they fed us, and fed us, and fed us. And we drank milk, and ate bread and fresh butter, and walked in the woods, and tried to put our life together. And that's where Ruda decided he'll go into medicine. And we went back to Prague. The first semester opened-- I think it was the 1st of August. So we had to be back in Prague. And I went back to school for-- for the year to finish my metric for this accelerated course, which took me a year. And-- and then, we slowly-- his friend came who was wonderful during the war to him who passed away in this May, and who were our closest friends all through the year, and visited us in Canada. And we always went to Prague to see them. And-- and-- and then, my father got back the factory. And-- and life was a bowl of cherries. We were-- we were on top of the world with our home. And then, Ruda's mother came back from Russia. She was a cook for a major there. And she had to run away from him. He wouldn't let her go. And she had a small apartment. And she had a boyfriend. And my father was chasing ladies as he did all his life. So he chased them again. And we had lots of money. That was no problem. And it was fine until February '48 when there was the first communist putsch. And we knew we had to leave. There was no other way. So we left. It took until November before we could leave. But we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: In March '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva, who did not come back from the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I-- I wrote it out for you. All my-- most of my cousins, my-- all my aunts and uncles, my mother, Ruda's father, all of-- all my cousins except for the cousin who is in London, all their children, because they were-- I was the youngest in the family. So they each had a child or two children-- nobody of those. Anna, the oldest cousin, she sent her daughter with this children's transport to England. And she survived, of course-- Anna. And Emil did not-- her husband-- they were in the March transport. We saw them in Auschwitz. And-- and Melanka, that's her name, she-- she's in-- she lives-- she lived in Canada in Edmonton. She had three children, but they moved next to England. Her husband is a physician-- a-- a researcher in biochemistry. But she-- he has an MD too. And they live partly in Paris and partly in London. And two of their children live in Canada. And one girl is in Manchester. She's a physician now and has a little boy-- married to an Indian. And I had three children. And then I had two children. And the children, my children and her children, love each other very much. They-- they are the family, you know-- the-- not brothers and sisters, they are the cousins and the uncles, I mean. When my cousin's husband died, and also my oldest son flew also to the funeral. And everybody was very unhappy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva, we're going to take a break.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. You still want something from me? What do you want from me? [LAUGHS]
